# tauri-syntax-permissions: Anti-Patterns

These are confirmed error patterns for the Tauri 2.x permissions and capabilities system. Each entry documents the WRONG pattern, the CORRECT pattern, and the WHY.

Sources:
- https://v2.tauri.app/security/permissions/
- https://v2.tauri.app/security/capabilities/
- vooronderzoek-tauri.md Section 5, Section 10.3, Section 10.5

---

## AP-001: Using v1 Allowlist Syntax

**WHY this is wrong**: The `allowlist` configuration was completely removed in Tauri 2. It does not exist in `tauri.conf.json`. Code generated by AI models frequently includes this v1 pattern, which is silently ignored or causes build errors.

```json
// WRONG — Tauri v1 syntax, does NOT exist in v2
{
  "tauri": {
    "allowlist": {
      "fs": {
        "readFile": true,
        "writeFile": true,
        "scope": ["$DOCUMENT/*"]
      },
      "dialog": {
        "open": true,
        "save": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```json
// CORRECT — Tauri v2: use capability files
// src-tauri/capabilities/default.json
{
  "$schema": "../gen/schemas/desktop-schema.json",
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "core:default",
    "fs:allow-read-file",
    "fs:allow-write-file",
    "dialog:allow-open",
    "dialog:allow-save"
  ]
}
```

NEVER use `allowlist` in Tauri 2 projects. ALWAYS use capability files in `src-tauri/capabilities/`.

---

## AP-002: Assuming Plugins Have Default Permissions

**WHY this is wrong**: Installing a plugin (adding the Rust crate to `Cargo.toml` and the npm package to `package.json`) does NOT automatically grant any permissions. Every plugin command requires explicit permission in a capability file. Without it, all plugin commands fail with "command not allowed" at runtime.

```rust
// Step 1 is done: plugin added to Cargo.toml and registered
tauri::Builder::default()
    .plugin(tauri_plugin_fs::init())
```

```typescript
// WRONG — no permissions configured, this throws at runtime
import { readTextFile } from '@tauri-apps/plugin-fs';
const content = await readTextFile('config.json');
// Error: "command not allowed"
```

```json
// CORRECT — add permissions to capability file
{
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "core:default",
    "fs:default",
    "fs:allow-read-file"
  ]
}
```

ALWAYS add permissions to a capability file after installing a plugin. Check plugin documentation for required permission identifiers.

---

## AP-003: Writing App Permissions in JSON Format

**WHY this is wrong**: Application-level permission definitions in `src-tauri/permissions/` MUST be in TOML format. JSON is only supported for capability files in `src-tauri/capabilities/`. Using JSON for permissions is silently ignored.

```json
// WRONG — src-tauri/permissions/my-commands.json (JSON not supported here)
{
  "permission": {
    "identifier": "allow-greet",
    "commands": {
      "allow": ["greet"]
    }
  }
}
```

```toml
# CORRECT — src-tauri/permissions/my-commands.toml (TOML required)
[[permission]]
identifier = "allow-greet"
description = "Allow the greet command"
commands.allow = ["greet"]
```

ALWAYS use TOML for permission definitions in `src-tauri/permissions/`. Capability files in `src-tauri/capabilities/` support both JSON and TOML.

---

## AP-004: Using Wildcard Windows with Broad Permissions

**WHY this is wrong**: `"windows": ["*"]` grants permissions to ALL windows, including any dynamically created ones. Combined with broad permissions like `fs:allow-write-file` or `shell:allow-execute`, this violates the principle of least privilege and creates security vulnerabilities.

```json
// WRONG — all windows get full filesystem access
{
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["*"],
  "permissions": [
    "fs:default",
    "fs:allow-read-file",
    "fs:allow-write-file",
    "shell:default",
    "shell:allow-execute"
  ]
}
```

```json
// CORRECT — specific windows with appropriate permissions
{
  "identifier": "main-capability",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "fs:default",
    "fs:allow-read-file",
    "fs:allow-write-file"
  ]
}
```

ALWAYS use specific window labels instead of `"*"` when granting sensitive permissions. Reserve `"*"` only for minimal core permissions like `core:event:default`.

---

## AP-005: Forgetting Core Permissions

**WHY this is wrong**: In Tauri 2, even core features like window management, events, and path resolution require explicit permissions. Without `core:default` or specific `core:*:default` entries, basic functionality like `getCurrentWindow()`, `emit()`, or `appDataDir()` will fail silently or throw errors.

```json
// WRONG — no core permissions, basic features broken
{
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "fs:default",
    "dialog:default"
  ]
}
```

```json
// CORRECT — include necessary core permissions
{
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "core:default",
    "core:window:default",
    "core:event:default",
    "core:path:default",
    "fs:default",
    "dialog:default"
  ]
}
```

ALWAYS include `core:default` (or the specific core module defaults you need) in every capability file.

---

## AP-006: Using Plugin Prefix for Custom App Commands

**WHY this is wrong**: Custom command permissions defined in `src-tauri/permissions/` do NOT use a plugin prefix. They are referenced by their identifier directly. Adding a prefix causes "permission not found" build errors.

```toml
# src-tauri/permissions/my-commands.toml
[[permission]]
identifier = "allow-greet"
commands.allow = ["greet"]
```

```json
// WRONG — custom app permissions have no plugin prefix
{
  "permissions": ["my-commands:allow-greet"]
}
```

```json
// CORRECT — use the identifier directly
{
  "permissions": ["allow-greet"]
}
```

NEVER use a prefix for custom app command permissions. Only plugin permissions use the `<plugin>:` prefix format.

---

## AP-007: Wrong Platform String Casing

**WHY this is wrong**: Platform strings in the `platforms` array are case-sensitive. Using wrong casing causes the capability to never match and permissions are silently not applied.

```json
// WRONG — incorrect casing
{
  "identifier": "desktop",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "platforms": ["Linux", "macos", "Windows"],
  "permissions": ["global-shortcut:allow-register"]
}
```

```json
// CORRECT — exact casing required
{
  "identifier": "desktop",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "platforms": ["linux", "macOS", "windows"],
  "permissions": ["global-shortcut:allow-register"]
}
```

ALWAYS use the exact casing: `"linux"`, `"macOS"`, `"windows"`, `"iOS"`, `"android"`. Note that `macOS` and `iOS` have specific capitalization.

---

## AP-008: Not Adding Capability File to capabilities/ Directory

**WHY this is wrong**: Capability files MUST be placed in `src-tauri/capabilities/` to be automatically discovered. Placing them elsewhere (e.g., in `src-tauri/` root or `src-tauri/permissions/`) means they are not loaded and permissions are not applied.

```
// WRONG — capability file in wrong location
src-tauri/
├── default.json              # NOT discovered here
├── permissions/
│   └── default.json          # This is for permission definitions, not capabilities
```

```
// CORRECT — capability file in capabilities/ directory
src-tauri/
├── capabilities/
│   └── default.json          # Auto-discovered and loaded
├── permissions/
│   └── my-commands.toml      # Permission definitions (TOML only)
```

ALWAYS place capability files in `src-tauri/capabilities/`. Place permission definitions in `src-tauri/permissions/`.

---

## AP-009: Granting Shell Execute Without Scope

**WHY this is wrong**: `shell:allow-execute` without scope restrictions allows arbitrary command execution from the frontend. This is a critical security vulnerability that effectively gives the frontend full system access.

```json
// WRONG — unrestricted shell access
{
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "shell:default",
    "shell:allow-execute"
  ]
}
```

```toml
# CORRECT — define a restricted scope first
# src-tauri/permissions/shell-scopes.toml
[[permission]]
identifier = "scope-safe-commands"
description = "Only allow specific commands"

[[scope.allow]]
name = "git"
cmd = "git"
args = ["status"]

[[scope.allow]]
name = "node"
cmd = "node"
args = ["--version"]
```

```json
// Then reference in capability
{
  "identifier": "default",
  "windows": ["main"],
  "permissions": [
    "shell:allow-execute",
    "scope-safe-commands"
  ]
}
```

ALWAYS define scope restrictions when granting shell execution permissions. Use `shell:allow-open` (which opens URLs/files with the default system app) as a safer alternative for most use cases.

---

## AP-010: Disabling CSP in Production

**WHY this is wrong**: Setting the Content Security Policy to `null` disables all content security restrictions, making the app vulnerable to XSS attacks. This is sometimes done during development and forgotten before release.

```json
// WRONG — CSP disabled
{
  "app": {
    "security": {
      "csp": null
    }
  }
}
```

```json
// CORRECT — restrictive CSP
{
  "app": {
    "security": {
      "csp": "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' asset: https://asset.localhost; connect-src ipc: http://ipc.localhost"
    }
  }
}
```

ALWAYS define a restrictive CSP in production. Only relax it for specific needs with the minimum required permissions.
